clinicalStem: A 34-year-old man was diagnosed with HIV infection 5 years ago but has been inconsistently taking antiretroviral therapy (ART). He now presents with a 3-week history of progressive dyspnoea, non-productive cough, and low-grade fever. His CD4 count is 68 cells/µL and viral load is 180,000 copies/mL. Chest X-ray shows bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities. LDH is elevated. He is started on co-trimoxazole and steroids. Which of the following best predicts his long-term prognosis if he consistently resumes ART after this episode?
options: Life expectancy approaches that of the general population with sustained viral suppression
He is unlikely to survive beyond 2 years regardless of ART adherence due to the AIDS-defining illness
CD4 count will never recover above 200 cells/µL once an AIDS-defining illness occurs
ART should be deferred for at least 6 months after resolution of this opportunistic infection
His prognosis is determined primarily by the viral subtype (clade) rather than treatment adherence
explanation: With consistent ART leading to sustained viral suppression, even patients who have had AIDS-defining illnesses can achieve near-normal life expectancy, as immune reconstitution occurs and CD4 counts recover over time. Modern ART can suppress viral load to undetectable levels, halting immune destruction. The key determinant of long-term prognosis in HIV is ART adherence and sustained virological suppression, not prior AIDS events.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — sustained viral suppression with ART normalises immune function and substantially extends life expectancy even after an AIDS-defining illness.
Incorrect — survival after AIDS-defining illness is not fixed at 2 years; consistent ART dramatically improves survival.
Incorrect — CD4 counts can and do recover above 200 cells/µL with effective ART, even after AIDS-defining illness.
Incorrect — current guidelines recommend initiating or resuming ART as soon as clinically stable (often within 2 weeks for PCP), not deferring for 6 months.
Incorrect — viral clade has minimal impact on individual prognosis compared to ART adherence and treatment response.
clinicalPearl: Consistent ART with sustained viral suppression is the single most important predictor of long-term prognosis in HIV, capable of restoring near-normal life expectancy even after AIDS-defining illnesses.
keyClue: Inconsistent ART with low CD4 and high viral load, now presenting with PCP (classic features); question asks about prognosis if ART is resumed.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that an AIDS-defining illness permanently worsens prognosis regardless of subsequent ART — once viral suppression is achieved, immune recovery is possible.
missedConcept: The reversibility of immune suppression with effective ART and the central role of treatment adherence in HIV prognosis.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with newly diagnosed HIV (CD4 count 45 cells/µL) was started on antiretroviral therapy 3 weeks ago. She had been treated for pulmonary tuberculosis 2 weeks before ART initiation. She now presents with high fever, worsening cervical lymphadenopathy, and new pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray. Her HIV viral load has dropped from 250,000 to 8,000 copies/mL since starting ART. TB cultures are still pending. What is the most likely explanation for her current deterioration?
options: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) triggered by TB
ART-induced drug-resistant tuberculosis
New bacterial superinfection due to ongoing immunosuppression
Antiretroviral drug hepatotoxicity causing systemic inflammation
Treatment failure due to non-adherence to ART
explanation: IRIS occurs when a recovering immune system mounts an exaggerated inflammatory response to a pre-existing pathogen (here, Mycobacterium tuberculosis) after ART initiation. The clinical picture of worsening TB symptoms within weeks of starting ART, paradoxically accompanied by a falling viral load (indicating treatment efficacy), is classic for unmasking or paradoxical IRIS. It is most common when ART is started with very low CD4 counts alongside active opportunistic infections.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — paradoxical IRIS after TB in the setting of early ART initiation with a very low baseline CD4 count, with evidence of virological response.
Incorrect — ART does not directly cause drug resistance in TB; resistance arises from inadequate TB treatment or non-adherence to TB drugs.
Incorrect — although she remains immunocompromised, the falling viral load and temporal relationship with ART initiation point to IRIS rather than a new superinfection.
Incorrect — hepatotoxicity would present with elevated liver enzymes and jaundice, not new lymphadenopathy and pulmonary infiltrates.
Incorrect — treatment failure would be reflected by a rising or unchanged viral load, not a significant decline.
clinicalPearl: IRIS classically presents within 4–8 weeks of ART initiation in patients with very low CD4 counts and active opportunistic infections; a falling viral load alongside worsening clinical features is the hallmark.
keyClue: Worsening TB symptoms 3 weeks after ART start, with a significantly declining viral load confirming ART is working.
commonExamTrap: Assuming clinical worsening always means ART failure — IRIS is a paradoxical worsening due to immune recovery, not treatment failure.
missedConcept: IRIS as a complication of ART initiation in the context of concurrent opportunistic infections and severely depressed CD4 counts.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with HIV has been on a first-line ART regimen containing tenofovir, lamivudine, and efavirenz for 18 months. He is virologically suppressed (viral load undetectable). He now presents with a 2-month history of bilateral lower limb weakness, numbness and tingling in his feet, and difficulty walking. His CD4 count is 520 cells/µL. Neurological examination confirms a stocking distribution sensory loss and reduced ankle reflexes bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in his management?
options: Review ART regimen for antiretroviral-associated peripheral neuropathy and consider substituting the offending agent
Start high-dose B12 supplementation alone as the most likely cause is nutritional deficiency
Initiate empirical anti-TB treatment for presumed spinal tuberculosis
Perform lumbar puncture to exclude HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis
Increase efavirenz dose to improve CNS viral suppression
explanation: Antiretroviral toxic neuropathy is a well-recognised complication of nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs), particularly stavudine (d4T) and didanosine (ddI), which inhibit mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma. Even tenofovir and lamivudine can rarely cause this. In a virologically suppressed patient on ART presenting with symmetric distal sensory neuropathy, the ART regimen should be reviewed and the likely culprit substituted. Continuing the offending drug risks irreversible nerve damage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — ART-associated peripheral neuropathy is the leading consideration in a virologically suppressed HIV patient on NRTIs with new symmetric distal sensory neuropathy.
Incorrect — while B12 deficiency is a cause of neuropathy, ART toxicity must be excluded first in an HIV patient on NRTIs, and B12 supplementation alone would delay appropriate management.
Incorrect — spinal TB would typically present with back pain, bladder/bowel involvement, or bony signs, not a pure distal stocking neuropathy.
Incorrect — cryptococcal meningitis presents with headache, photophobia, and meningismus; a CD4 of 520 cells/µL makes opportunistic CNS infection unlikely.
Incorrect — increasing efavirenz dose does not treat peripheral neuropathy and may increase CNS side effects such as vivid dreams and dizziness.
clinicalPearl: Distal symmetric peripheral neuropathy in an HIV patient on NRTIs should prompt a review of the ART regimen for drug-induced mitochondrial toxicity, even in virologically suppressed patients.
keyClue: Virologically suppressed on NRTI-containing ART with new bilateral distal stocking-distribution neuropathy and reduced ankle reflexes.
commonExamTrap: Attributing neuropathy to HIV itself or nutritional deficiency without first considering ART toxicity as the cause in a suppressed patient.
missedConcept: Antiretroviral toxic neuropathy as a long-term complication of NRTI-based ART requiring regimen modification.

topic: Viral haemorrhagic fever — prognosis and complications
clinicalStem: During an Ebola virus disease (EVD) outbreak, a 38-year-old healthcare worker develops fever, severe headache, myalgia, vomiting, and profuse watery diarrhoea on day 3 of illness. By day 6 he develops haematemesis, melena, and petechiae. He is admitted to an Ebola treatment unit. His blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 128 bpm, and he is oliguric. Which single factor most strongly predicts a poor prognosis in this patient?
options: Development of haemorrhagic manifestations with haemodynamic shock and oliguria
Symptom onset occurring 3 days after likely exposure
Healthcare worker occupation
Presence of headache and myalgia in the first 3 days
Age of 38 years in an otherwise healthy adult
explanation: In Ebola virus disease, haemorrhagic manifestations combined with hypovolaemic shock and organ failure (oliguria indicating renal impairment) are the strongest predictors of fatal outcome. The case fatality rate in EVD is high (25–90% depending on the strain and healthcare setting), and patients who develop shock, multi-organ failure, and haemorrhage have significantly worse outcomes than those who do not. Early aggressive supportive care with fluid resuscitation can improve survival.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — haemorrhagic manifestations with haemodynamic shock and oliguria (indicating multi-organ involvement) are the most powerful prognostic markers of fatal EVD.
Incorrect — the incubation period of 2–21 days for EVD means day 3 onset is within the normal range and does not independently predict prognosis.
Incorrect — while healthcare workers are at higher exposure risk, occupation itself does not alter disease prognosis once infected.
Incorrect — fever, headache, and myalgia in the first few days are universal features of EVD and do not independently predict mortality.
Incorrect — age 38 is not an independent negative prognostic factor; haemodynamic status and organ failure are far stronger determinants of outcome.
clinicalPearl: In viral haemorrhagic fevers, the onset of haemorrhage combined with shock and organ failure (renal, hepatic) signals imminent multi-organ failure and is the strongest predictor of mortality.
keyClue: Haematemesis, melena, petechiae, hypotension, tachycardia, and oliguria — haemorrhage with multi-organ involvement.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the specific virus type or occupation rather than recognising haemodynamic instability and organ failure as the key prognostic determinants.
missedConcept: Multi-organ failure and haemorrhage as the dominant prognostic indicators in viral haemorrhagic fevers.
redFlagReason: Haemodynamic shock (BP 85/50, HR 128) with oliguria and active haemorrhage represent an immediately life-threatening emergency requiring urgent resuscitation.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman is discharged from hospital after recovering from confirmed Lassa fever in a West African clinic. She received ribavirin treatment and her fever resolved after 10 days. Two weeks after discharge, she returns to clinic complaining of bilateral hearing loss that has progressively worsened since she left hospital. She denies ear pain, discharge, or any recent ear infection. On examination, whisper test is failed bilaterally and otoscopy is normal. What is the most likely explanation for her hearing loss?
options: Lassa fever sensorineural hearing loss as a recognised late complication
Ribavirin ototoxicity causing bilateral cochlear damage
Acute otitis media secondary to post-illness immunosuppression
Functional (psychogenic) hearing loss related to the trauma of illness
Noise-induced hearing loss from the hospital environment
explanation: Sensorineural hearing loss is one of the most important and well-documented late complications of Lassa fever, occurring in approximately 25–30% of survivors. It can be sudden, severe, and bilateral, and is thought to result from direct viral damage to the cochlea and auditory nerve. Importantly, it occurs even in patients who receive ribavirin treatment and recover clinically, making it a key follow-up concern for all Lassa survivors.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — Lassa fever sensorineural hearing loss is a recognised late complication affecting up to 30% of survivors, even in those successfully treated with ribavirin.
Incorrect — ribavirin is not known to be ototoxic; aminoglycosides and loop diuretics are the classic drug causes of sensorineural hearing loss.
Incorrect — acute otitis media would present with ear pain, conductive (not sensorineural) hearing loss pattern, and abnormal otoscopy findings.
Incorrect — functional hearing loss would typically be inconsistent on formal audiometry and is a diagnosis of exclusion; the clinical context strongly points to Lassa fever complication.
Incorrect — noise-induced hearing loss develops over years of exposure; a 2-week course in hospital does not produce bilateral sensorineural loss.
clinicalPearl: Sensorineural hearing loss affects up to 30% of Lassa fever survivors and can occur despite clinical recovery and ribavirin treatment — all recovered patients should have audiological follow-up.
keyClue: Bilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss in a Lassa fever survivor with normal otoscopy 2 weeks after discharge.
commonExamTrap: Attributing hearing loss to ribavirin ototoxicity — ribavirin is not ototoxic, and this is a recognised complication of the virus itself.
missedConcept: Lassa fever sensorineural hearing loss as a common and specific post-infection complication requiring active follow-up.

topic: Viral haemorrhagic fever — public health and follow-up
clinicalStem: A 29-year-old male Ebola virus disease survivor, who recovered clinically 3 months ago, presents to a primary health care clinic with his female partner who is 8 weeks pregnant. He has been clinically well since recovery. He is concerned because he has heard that Ebola virus can persist in certain body fluids. His partner asks whether she is at risk of acquiring EVD from her husband. What is the most accurate information to give this couple?
options: Ebola virus can persist in semen for many months after clinical recovery, and protected sex or abstinence should be advised until semen tests negative
The patient is no longer infectious and there is no risk to his partner once he has clinically recovered
Ebola virus persists only in blood and poses no risk via sexual transmission
The partner requires post-exposure prophylaxis immediately given the pregnancy
Ebola survivors must remain in isolation for 12 months post-recovery before resuming normal relationships
explanation: Ebola virus can persist in immune-privileged sites such as the testes, eyes, and central nervous system for prolonged periods after clinical recovery. Semen has been shown to contain viable Ebola virus for up to 18 months or longer after illness, and sexual transmission from male survivors to partners has been documented. WHO guidelines recommend male EVD survivors practice safe sex (condoms) or abstinence until two consecutive semen tests (taken at least one week apart) are negative by RT-PCR.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — EVD viral persistence in semen is well-documented for months to over a year post-recovery; protected sex is essential until semen tests negative.
Incorrect — clinical recovery does not equate to complete viral clearance; Ebola persists in immune-privileged sites and semen long after symptoms resolve.
Incorrect — Ebola is not limited to blood after recovery; semen viral persistence is the most clinically significant route of post-recovery transmission.
Incorrect — there is no currently approved post-exposure prophylaxis for the partner; the correct action is safe sex and semen testing for the survivor.
Incorrect — 12-month isolation is not required; guidance is based on semen PCR negativity, not a fixed isolation period.
clinicalPearl: Male Ebola survivors can have detectable and infectious virus in semen for 18+ months post-recovery; sexual transmission is documented and safe sex/abstinence until semen clears is mandatory.
keyClue: Male EVD survivor 3 months post-recovery with pregnant partner — viral persistence in semen is the key safety concern.
commonExamTrap: Assuming clinical recovery equals full infectivity clearance — Ebola is an exception where viral persistence in immune-privileged sites continues long after resolution of symptoms.
missedConcept: Ebola virus persistence in semen as a post-recovery sexual transmission risk requiring specific follow-up testing and safe sex counselling.

clinicalStem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to the primary health care centre by her mother with a 2-day history of profuse watery diarrhoea (8–10 stools per day) and vomiting. On examination she is lethargic, has sunken eyes, a very dry mouth, no tears when crying, a skin pinch that goes back very slowly (>2 seconds), and a weak rapid pulse. Her weight today is 8.5 kg; her pre-illness weight recorded last month was 9.8 kg. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Initiate Plan C rehydration with intravenous Ringer's lactate 100 mL/kg over 3 hours
Start Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) at 75 mL/kg over 4 hours (Plan B) and observe
Advise the mother to continue breastfeeding and give ORS at home (Plan A)
Administer oral metronidazole for presumed Giardia lamblia infection
Withhold all fluids until stool microscopy confirms the organism
explanation: This child has signs of severe dehydration (lethargy, sunken eyes, very dry mouth, skin pinch >2 seconds, weak rapid pulse) by WHO criteria, which requires Plan C — urgent IV rehydration. The WHO Plan C protocol for children 1 month to 5 years is Ringer's lactate (or normal saline) 100 mL/kg: 30 mL/kg in the first 30 minutes, then 70 mL/kg over the next 2.5 hours. Delaying IV fluids in severe dehydration risks hypovolaemic shock and death.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — severe dehydration by WHO criteria (lethargy, skin pinch >2 sec, very dry mouth, weak rapid pulse) mandates immediate IV rehydration with Plan C.
Incorrect — Plan B (ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours) is for some dehydration, not severe dehydration; oral rehydration is insufficient when the child is lethargic with shock signs.
Incorrect — Plan A (home ORS) is for no dehydration; this child has severe dehydration and requires immediate facility-based IV treatment.
Incorrect — antibiotic therapy is not the immediate priority; correction of life-threatening dehydration and electrolyte imbalance takes precedence.
Incorrect — withholding fluids pending laboratory confirmation is dangerous and delays potentially life-saving rehydration in severe dehydration.
clinicalPearl: In severe paediatric dehydration (lethargy, skin pinch >2 sec, weak rapid pulse), immediate IV rehydration with Plan C (Ringer's lactate 100 mL/kg) is lifesaving and must not be delayed for investigations.
keyClue: Lethargy, skin pinch >2 seconds, weak rapid pulse, and very dry mouth in a toddler with profuse watery diarrhoea = severe dehydration requiring Plan C.
commonExamTrap: Starting oral rehydration (Plan B) in a lethargic child with shock signs — severe dehydration requires IV fluids, not ORS.
missedConcept: WHO dehydration classification and the critical decision to escalate from ORS to IV fluids in severe dehydration.
redFlagReason: Severe dehydration with weak rapid pulse and lethargy indicates impending hypovolaemic shock — delayed IV resuscitation can be fatal in young children.

clinicalStem: A 22-month-old boy presents to the primary health care clinic with diarrhoea that has lasted 18 days. His mother reports 4–5 loose stools daily. He has had no blood in stools. Examination reveals weight-for-age below -3 SD (severe acute malnutrition), bilateral pitting oedema up to the knees, and angular stomatitis. He has no signs of dehydration. His mother says he started having diarrhoea shortly after a 3-week course of oral amoxicillin for a chest infection. Which of the following best describes the expected clinical course if persistent diarrhoea is left untreated in this setting?
options: Progressive nutritional deterioration and increased mortality risk due to the vicious cycle of persistent diarrhoea and malnutrition
Spontaneous resolution within 7 days once the antibiotics have fully cleared from his system
Development of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) as the primary long-term complication
Full recovery once zinc supplementation alone is initiated
Persistent diarrhoea lasting more than 14 days carries no additional mortality risk compared with acute diarrhoea
explanation: Persistent diarrhoea (lasting 14 days or more) in the context of malnutrition creates a dangerous bidirectional relationship: diarrhoea worsens nutritional status through malabsorption and catabolism, and malnutrition impairs gut immune defences and mucosal integrity, perpetuating diarrhoea. This cycle is associated with significantly increased case fatality rates — persistent diarrhoea accounts for a disproportionate fraction of diarrhoea-related child deaths. Treatment requires addressing both nutritional rehabilitation and the diarrhoea itself.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — the diarrhoea-malnutrition cycle in persistent diarrhoea is a major driver of child mortality and will not resolve without active nutritional and medical management.
Incorrect — antibiotic-associated diarrhoea may contribute, but the child's severe malnutrition means spontaneous resolution is unlikely without nutritional rehabilitation.
Incorrect — IBD is a chronic relapsing inflammatory condition not caused by post-infectious or malnutrition-related persistent diarrhoea in this context.
Incorrect — zinc supplementation reduces diarrhoea duration and severity but alone is insufficient to reverse severe acute malnutrition with oedema (kwashiorkor).
Incorrect — persistent diarrhoea carries substantially higher mortality than acute diarrhoea, particularly in malnourished children.
clinicalPearl: Persistent diarrhoea (≥14 days) in a malnourished child perpetuates a deadly cycle of worsening nutritional status and immune failure, accounting for a disproportionate share of diarrhoea-related child deaths.
keyClue: 18 days of diarrhoea (persistent) in a severely malnourished child with oedema — the combination predicts a progressive, high-mortality trajectory if untreated.
commonExamTrap: Expecting spontaneous resolution of persistent diarrhoea in a malnourished child or treating only one component (diarrhoea or malnutrition) without addressing both.
missedConcept: The bidirectional relationship between persistent diarrhoea and malnutrition, and the elevated mortality risk this combination carries.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl presents with a 4-day history of bloody diarrhoea and crampy abdominal pain. She was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole by her family doctor 2 days ago. Today she is brought back because she is pale, barely passing urine, and markedly irritable. Her conjunctivae are very pale. Blood tests reveal: haemoglobin 6.2 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, creatinine 280 µmol/L, and peripheral smear shows schistocytes. What complication has developed, and what is the most important management implication?
options: Haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS) — antibiotics (particularly those triggering Shiga toxin release) may worsen HUS and should be stopped; refer urgently for renal support
Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — urgent fresh frozen plasma and platelets are required
Antibiotic-induced aplastic anaemia — stop antibiotics and start colony-stimulating factor
Visceral leishmaniasis presenting with pancytopaenia — initiate amphotericin B
Iron deficiency anaemia from chronic blood loss — start oral iron supplementation
explanation: This child has the classic triad of HUS: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (pallor, Hb 6.2, schistocytes), thrombocytopaenia, and acute renal failure (elevated creatinine, oliguria). HUS in children most commonly follows Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli (STEC, typically O157:H7) infection causing bloody diarrhoea. Critically, antibiotics — especially those that trigger increased Shiga toxin release (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX) — are associated with worse HUS outcomes. Management is supportive, with urgent renal referral for dialysis if needed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — the triad of microangiopathic haemolysis (schistocytes), thrombocytopaenia, and acute kidney injury in a child with bloody diarrhoea = HUS; antibiotics should be stopped urgently.
Incorrect — DIC would show prolonged PT/APTT, elevated D-dimers, and fibrinogen depletion; the isolated triad here (schistocytes, low platelets, renal failure) fits HUS much better.
Incorrect — aplastic anaemia affects all cell lines through bone marrow suppression and takes weeks to develop; schistocytes indicate haemolytic anaemia, not aplasia.
Incorrect — visceral leishmaniasis causes hepatosplenomegaly and pancytopaenia in endemic areas but is not associated with acute bloody diarrhoea and schistocytes.
Incorrect — iron deficiency anaemia does not cause schistocytes, thrombocytopaenia, or acute renal failure; this picture is incompatible with simple iron deficiency.
clinicalPearl: In children with post-dysenteric HUS, antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones and TMP-SMX) may worsen outcomes by promoting Shiga toxin release — supportive care and renal referral, not antibiotics, is the priority.
keyClue: Bloody diarrhoea + microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes) + thrombocytopaenia + acute renal failure = HUS triad.
commonExamTrap: Continuing or adding antibiotics in bloody diarrhoea without recognising that HUS has developed — antibiotics are harmful in STEC-associated HUS.
missedConcept: HUS as a serious complication of Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) dysentery, and the counterintuitive harm of antibiotics in this condition.
redFlagReason: Acute renal failure with haemolytic anaemia and thrombocytopaenia (HUS) is an emergency requiring urgent renal support — delayed recognition can lead to irreversible kidney damage or death.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman with a known peanut allergy attends the primary health care clinic for a routine follow-up. While in the waiting room, she inadvertently eats a biscuit that contains peanuts. Five minutes later she develops generalised urticaria, tongue swelling, stridor, and increasing dyspnoea. Her blood pressure is 88/58 mmHg and heart rate is 130 bpm. Which single intervention should be administered first?
options: Intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the outer mid-thigh
Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg
Oral cetirizine 10 mg
Nebulised salbutamol for bronchospasm
IV fluid bolus with normal saline 500 mL
explanation: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening emergency and intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine) is the first-line and most important treatment. It counteracts vasodilation, bronchospasm, and mucosal oedema through its alpha and beta adrenergic effects. The IM route (outer mid-thigh) allows faster absorption than subcutaneous. All other interventions (antihistamines, steroids, nebulisers, IV fluids) are adjunctive and should only follow adrenaline administration, never replace it.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IM adrenaline is the first and most critical intervention in anaphylaxis; it is the only treatment shown to reduce mortality.
Incorrect — IV hydrocortisone is an adjunct used after adrenaline to reduce the risk of biphasic reactions; it has a slow onset of action and does not reverse acute anaphylaxis.
Incorrect — oral cetirizine is a non-sedating antihistamine; antihistamines treat urticaria and pruritus but do not reverse airway compromise or shock in anaphylaxis.
Incorrect — nebulised salbutamol may help relieve bronchospasm as an adjunct but does not address cardiovascular collapse or upper airway oedema — adrenaline must come first.
Incorrect — IV fluids support blood pressure in distributive shock but are adjunctive; adrenaline must be given first to reverse the primary pathophysiology.
clinicalPearl: In anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline into the outer mid-thigh is always the first treatment — antihistamines, steroids, and nebulisers are adjuncts that follow, never substitutes.
keyClue: Urticaria + tongue swelling + stridor + hypotension + tachycardia after known allergen exposure = anaphylaxis requiring immediate IM adrenaline.
commonExamTrap: Giving antihistamines or steroids first — these are adjuncts; adrenaline is the only drug that reverses the life-threatening cardiovascular and airway components of anaphylaxis.
missedConcept: Adrenaline as the mandatory first-line treatment in anaphylaxis, before all other agents.
redFlagReason: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise (stridor, tongue swelling) and haemodynamic shock (BP 88/58) is immediately life-threatening — delay in adrenaline administration can be fatal.

clinicalStem: A 10-year-old boy with a 4-year history of intermittent wheeze and cough is diagnosed with moderate persistent asthma. He is started on an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) plus a short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) as needed. At his 3-month review, his mother reports he still wakes up 3 times per week with cough and has needed his reliever inhaler on most days. His peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) is 68% of predicted. Inhaler technique and adherence have been confirmed as correct. What is the most appropriate next step in his management?
options: Step up therapy by adding a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) to the existing inhaled corticosteroid
Switch the inhaled corticosteroid to oral prednisolone for 4 weeks and then reassess
Discharge from follow-up as nocturnal symptoms are normal in childhood asthma
Stop the ICS and manage with SABA alone as he is too young for combination therapy
Continue current therapy for a further 6 months and repeat spirometry
explanation: This child has uncontrolled moderate persistent asthma despite adequate ICS monotherapy, confirmed by nocturnal symptoms (>1 night/week), frequent SABA use, and PEFR 68% of predicted. According to GINA stepwise management, the appropriate response is to step up therapy. In children ≥5 years, adding a LABA to ICS (combination inhaler) is the preferred step-up strategy at step 3. Continuing unchanged therapy when asthma is uncontrolled risks worsening lung function decline and reduces long-term prognosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — stepping up to ICS + LABA (step 3) is appropriate for uncontrolled moderate persistent asthma with confirmed adherence and correct technique.
Incorrect — switching to oral prednisolone is reserved for acute exacerbations, not for regular maintenance step-up; long-term oral steroids carry significant systemic adverse effects.
Incorrect — nocturnal symptoms more than once per week define uncontrolled asthma and represent a significant prognosis and quality-of-life concern requiring active intervention.
Incorrect — withholding ICS and relying on SABA alone is inappropriate for moderate persistent asthma and greatly increases the risk of severe exacerbations.
Incorrect — continuing inadequate therapy for 6 more months in uncontrolled asthma delays appropriate escalation and risks progressive airway remodelling.
clinicalPearl: Uncontrolled asthma despite adequate ICS and correct inhaler technique mandates stepping up therapy (add LABA) to prevent progressive airway remodelling and maintain long-term lung function.
keyClue: Nocturnal symptoms >1x/week, daily SABA use, and PEFR 68% predicted despite ICS with confirmed correct technique = uncontrolled asthma requiring step-up.
commonExamTrap: Continuing the same therapy because the child 'is on treatment' without recognising that uncontrolled asthma requires active step-up per GINA guidelines.
missedConcept: GINA stepwise approach: uncontrolled asthma on ICS monotherapy requires addition of LABA (not just continued observation or oral steroids).

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old man presents to the primary health care clinic with a 5-year history of perennial nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhoea, sneezing, and nasal itching. He also reports frequent frontal headaches and a sensation of pressure over his cheeks and forehead, which worsens with bending forward. He has no fever. His nasal mucosa is pale and boggy on examination. He uses an oral decongestant daily. Which of the following best explains the complication this patient has most likely developed?
options: Chronic rhinosinusitis secondary to persistent mucosal oedema and impaired sinus drainage from allergic rhinitis
Migraine headache unrelated to his nasal disease
Bacterial meningitis presenting with headache and nasal symptoms
Rhinitis medicamentosa from overuse of an intranasal decongestant
Nasal polyposis causing cavernous sinus thrombosis
explanation: Allergic rhinitis causes chronic mucosal inflammation and oedema, which impairs the ostio-meatal complex drainage of the paranasal sinuses. Over time, this predisposes to recurrent and chronic rhinosinusitis, the most common local complication of untreated or poorly controlled allergic rhinitis. The classic features of pressure-type facial pain worsening on bending forward, maxillary and frontal distribution, and no fever in the context of longstanding allergic rhinitis support this diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — chronic rhinosinusitis is the most common local complication of allergic rhinitis, caused by mucosal oedema obstructing sinus drainage.
Incorrect — migraine is unilateral, pulsating, and associated with photophobia/phonophobia/nausea; the positional facial pressure and nasal symptoms here indicate a sinonasal cause.
Incorrect — bacterial meningitis would present with fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and altered consciousness — not 5 years of gradual nasal symptoms.

Incorrect — rhinitis medicamentosa is caused by overuse of intranasal decongestant sprays (oxymetazoline), not oral decongestants; it presents with rebound nasal congestion, not sinus pressure headaches.
Incorrect — cavernous sinus thrombosis is a rare and severe complication presenting with proptosis, chemosis, cranial nerve palsies, and high fever — not consistent with this presentation.
clinicalPearl: Chronic rhinosinusitis is the most common local complication of allergic rhinitis; persistent mucosal inflammation obstructs sinus ostia and leads to pressure facial pain worsening on bending.
keyClue: 5 years of perennial allergic rhinitis + facial pressure headache worsening on bending forward + pale boggy mucosa = chronic rhinosinusitis complication.
commonExamTrap: Attributing sinus pressure headaches to migraine or tension headache without considering rhinosinusitis as a direct complication of the longstanding allergic rhinitis.
missedConcept: Allergic rhinitis as a driver of chronic rhinosinusitis through sinus ostial obstruction — the local complications of untreated allergic rhinitis.
